question: In the context of the Central Limit Theorem, what is the relationship between the variance of the population distribution and the variance of the sampling distribution of the sample mean? Explain why this relationship is fundamental to statistical inference.
expectedPoints: The variance of the sampling distribution of the mean (σ²ₓ̄) is equal to the population variance (σ²) divided by the sample size (n): σ²ₓ̄ = σ²/n.
This relationship shows that the distribution of sample means becomes more concentrated around the true population mean as the sample size increases.
This reduction in variability is why larger samples yield more precise estimates of the population mean, forming the basis for constructing confidence intervals and conducting hypothesis tests.

question: Describe the primary visual characteristics you would expect to see when plotting the distribution of sample means drawn from a non-normal, right-skewed population distribution. How does the sample size affect this plot?
expectedPoints: For a small sample size (e.g., n=5), the distribution of sample means will likely also be right-skewed, resembling the original population.
As the sample size increases (e.g., n=30 or n=50), the distribution will become more symmetric and bell-shaped, adhering to the Central Limit Theorem.
The mean of the sampling distribution will always be equal to the population mean (μ), regardless of the sample size or the shape of the original distribution.

question: What is the standard error of the mean and what does its value tell you about the plotted distribution of sample means?
expectedPoints: The standard error of the mean (SEM) is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution of the sample mean.
It quantifies the average distance or variability between the sample means (x̄) and the true population mean (μ).
A smaller SEM indicates that the sample means are clustered more tightly around μ, resulting in a narrower, taller plot of the distribution.
